bull_thesis:
Globant (GLOB) is a deep-value case on cash flow, priced as if the business is in permanent decline — which the numbers do not support. At $42.57 the stock trades at 6.5 times its price-to-free-cash-flow ratio (that is, the share price divided by the free cash flow, or cash profit, the company generates per share) against trailing twelve months (TTM, meaning the most recent four quarters) free cash flow (FCF) of $303 million — a 10.6% FCF margin that grew 18% year over year. The company has operated as a nearshore digital-engineering firm for 22 years, serving marquee clients including OpenAI, AWS, NVIDIA (as a global partner), Riot Games, LaLiga, and Natura.

The market is treating a cyclical technology-services slowdown and the risk of Globant's Argentine domicile as permanent impairments. The reality is more nuanced: revenue is essentially flat at +1.6% in U.S. dollars year over year, but constant-currency growth was still +8.6% in Q1 2025. The sales pipeline hit successive all-time highs ($3.4–3.7 billion, with bookings up 32.4% in the fourth quarter of 2025). First-quarter 2026 results (reported May 14, 2026) beat guidance — revenue of $607.1 million, non-IFRS earnings per share of $1.50 against a consensus estimate of $1.44 — and full-year 2026 guidance was maintained (14–15% adjusted operating margin, $6.10–6.50 adjusted earnings per share). The pattern of serial guidance cuts appears to have ended.

The strategic optionality is AI Pods, a token-subscription delivery model that earns 45–60% gross margins versus the blended 38% on traditional seat-based work. Annual recurring revenue (ARR, the annualized value of subscription contracts) compounded from $20.6 million at year-end 2025 to $32.8 million in Q1 2026, with a company target of $60–100 million by the end of 2026. If it scales, it is structurally margin-accretive and repositions Globant as an AI-native services company.

Capital allocation is shareholder-friendly: management authorized two separate buyback programs — one in Q3 2025 and a fresh $125 million program ($50 million per quarter through Q4 2027, announced May 14, 2026) — repurchasing shares at a trough valuation. A margin of safety (the gap between our estimate of fair value and the current price) analysis using three independent methods all agree the stock is cheap: discounted cash flow +41%, owner earnings +46%, and earnings power value (EPV, a conservative valuation that assumes no growth) +19%.

For this thesis to hold, three conditions must remain true: enterprise technology-services demand stabilizes without a deeper recession; generative AI does not erode the core labor-arbitrage business faster than AI Pods can replace it; and Argentine peso volatility remains contained.
bear_thesis:
The bull case rests on a pivot that represents less than 1.5% of revenue, and on a cash-flow multiple that may be cheap for good reason.

Earnings per share collapsed 38.4% year over year ($3.72 to $2.29) and net margin compressed to 4.2% — the profitability base eroded far faster than the relentless 'all-time-high pipeline' commentary implies. Three consecutive quarters of record pipeline produced essentially flat revenue, which suggests either the pipeline-to-revenue conversion process is broken or the new bookings are low-margin.

The core existential risk is that generative AI permanently deflates the per-seat, labor-arbitrage IT-services model. Gartner projects IT-services sector growth of just 4.4% for 2026 — roughly half the overall technology industry — and management openly concedes cannibalization: 'I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization… I want that transformation to happen.' Yet on three consecutive earnings calls, management declined to quantify the revenue crossover point — the moment at which AI Pods revenue offsets what the core business loses to AI automation. That is the single most important modeling question, and it remains unanswered. AI Pods at $32.8 million ARR is immaterial against $2.45 billion in total revenue; even if Globant hits the top of its $60–100 million 2026 target, that is still only 3–4% of revenue.

Governance and candor are impaired. A securities class action — covering the period February 2024 through August 2025, with a lead-plaintiff deadline of June 23, 2026 — alleges this same management team concealed deterioration in Latin American operations while publicly describing the region as 'particularly beneficial.' The stock already fell from $210 in February 2025 to $66 in August 2025 on three corrective disclosures; it now sits at $42. Corrected open-market insider transaction data shows 17 buys (146,000 shares) versus 16 sells (21.4 million shares) — insiders are heavy net sellers even as the company repurchases stock.

Argentine domicile adds currency and political risk, and wage-freeze exposure cited in the litigation. Net debt of $262 million is a liability, not a cash cushion. This is a falling knife where a cheap multiple could get cheaper if 2026 revenue growth remains negative. The bear case is confirmed by: another revenue guidance cut, AI Pods ARR growth stalling below the $60 million floor, or renewed Latin American deterioration.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market: the central question for a deep-value stock is whether the market is pricing permanent decline into a company whose cash earnings power is actually stable or growing. Here the answer is partly yes — and partly justified.

The headline consensus price target of roughly $78 is stale and lags reality. The more current signal is the May 2026 downgrade wave — Truist at $44, Needham at $50, TD Cowen at $59, Wedbush at $54 — representing an analyst-target price decline of roughly 11%, which has finally converged to the $42 share price.

The genuinely mispriced fact is free cash flow. The market values Globant at 6.5 times $303 million in TTM FCF as though that cash flow is in structural decline — but FCF actually grew 18% year over year, and Q1 2026 beat estimates with full-year guidance maintained. The street is conflating flat reported revenue (real) with collapsing cash generation (false).

Conversely, the street is not wrong to discount the AI Pods narrative. At less than 1.5% of revenue, with the revenue crossover point unquantified, paying up for the 'AWS-moment' framing would be the mistake. The genuine opportunity is narrow and specific: own the cheap, growing, buyback-supported cash flow; do not pay for the pivot story. The mistaken street assumption to fade is 'Globant's cash earnings power is impaired' — it is not yet. The caution worth respecting is 'the growth and AI-deflation question is unresolved.'
forcing_function:
The catalyst that forces the re-rating: there is no single hard-dated event — and that is the defining weakness of this setup. The candidate drivers, ranked by reliability:

1. The $125 million buyback ($50 million per quarter through Q4 2027) provides a continuous, management-controlled bid that shrinks the share count at a trough valuation. It is the most reliable convergence driver, but slow — roughly 1.1 million shares per quarter, or about 2.5% of the float per year.

2. Q2 2026 earnings (expected around August 2026) is the next proof point on whether full-year guidance holds and whether AI Pods ARR is tracking toward the $60–100 million floor. A maintained guide plus on-track ARR would validate that the guidance-cut cycle has ended.

3. June 23, 2026 — the litigation lead-plaintiff deadline — is a near-term headline event that clears (or escalates) an overhang, but is not a positive catalyst in itself.

4. AI Pods crossing a materiality threshold (roughly $60 million or more in ARR, the 2026 exit target) would be the structural re-rating catalyst, but that is a year away and depends on discretionary enterprise spending.

None of these is a dated, binding, contractually asymmetric event of the kind that would qualify as a hard catalyst.
valley_of_death:
The risky window: near-term hazards over the next three to nine months are real, though none is balance-sheet-threatening.

1. Litigation overhang: the securities class-action lead-plaintiff deadline on June 23, 2026 (days away) will generate a fresh news cycle and headline risk. The suit covers a past period (February 2024 through August 2025) and is a reputational and legal-cost risk rather than an existential claim — but it caps multiple expansion until resolved.

2. Seasonal cash-flow pattern: management noted that Globant 'consumes cash in the first half and generates in the second half.' First-half FCF optics are structurally weak, and a soft Q2 2026 print (expected around August 2026) could re-test the share price lows.

3. Macro and AI capital expenditure exposure: the current market regime analysis shows AI capital spending accounts for roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings-per-share growth and is the dominant shared factor across many portfolios. Globant's re-rating thesis (enterprise AI-services demand recovers) is directly levered to that same wave — so any AI-capex scare that causes fund managers to reduce risk would pressure Globant precisely when its pipeline-to-revenue conversion story needs spending to recover.

4. No hard-dated catalyst exists to force a re-rating in the interim.

Net debt of $262 million is modest against $303 million in TTM FCF (comfortably covered), and a $725 million credit facility provides liquidity with no near-term maturity wall. The risky window is a slow grind with headline risk — not a solvency cliff.
positioning_washout:
Forced-selling pressure: the mechanical de-positioning is largely complete, and the residual forced-selling risk is modest.

Globant does not pay a dividend, so there is no income-investor base that would be forced to exit on a dividend cut. The heavy selling already happened: the stock fell roughly 80% from $210 in February 2025 to $66 in August 2025 on three corrective disclosures, and has since drifted to $42 — ejecting growth-momentum holders and environmental-social-governance (ESG) and Latin America-growth-focused investors along the way. The stock sits only 8% above its 52-week low, and the smart-money positioning score of 29 out of 100 confirms that institutional positioning is already washed out rather than primed for further liquidation.

The remaining forced-selling risk is factor-driven: continued analyst price-target cuts (the recent velocity is -11%) and potential further downgrades could pressure quantitative and momentum-oriented funds; a securities-suit escalation could trigger exits from governance-screen portfolios. But the heaviest de-positioning work is done. That argues the asymmetric pain trade is now to the upside (short-covering), not the downside — which supports a watchlist-and-wait stance rather than aggressive buying at current prices.
moderator_conclusion:
Globant is a legitimately cheap, cash-generative, buyback-supported deep-value name whose value case is strong but whose catalyst is soft, single-axis, and undated — so the two assessments diverge.

The quality of the financials is medium-good. FCF is honest and growing (+18% year over year, $303 million TTM, 6.5 times price-to-FCF), Q1 2026 beat with full-year guidance maintained, management has ended the serial guidance-cut pattern, and capital allocation is aligned (two buybacks at a trough multiple).

The negatives are equally real: earnings per share down 38%, a 4.2% net margin, a broken pipeline-to-revenue conversion process, an unquantified AI-cannibalization crossover that management refuses to model, a live securities class action (lead-plaintiff deadline June 23, 2026) impugning this team's past candor, and heavy open-market insider selling (21.4 million shares sold versus 146,000 bought) that flatly contradicts the buyback's message.

Applying the current market regime overlay: the regime favors structural special situations whose re-rating driver is independent of broad market factors, and penalizes theses that hinge on a single shared macro driver. Globant's re-rating depends squarely on enterprise AI-services spending — the same AI capital-expenditure wave that accounts for roughly 40% of index earnings-per-share growth and is the dominant shared factor across portfolios. It fails the independence test and earns no regime tilt. Its catalyst is a dated but soft catalyst with no contractual payout (a serially extended ARR ramp plus macro-conversion hope, no hard date), which reduces conviction.

A sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) base-case fair value of $55–60 (anchored to FCF, net-debt-adjusted, cross-checked against earnings power value) versus the current $42.57 implies roughly 30–41% upside to base case and 70–88% to the bull case, against roughly 15–20% downside to the bear-case break-even — a favorable asymmetry, but one that converges slowly with headline risk into late June.

The recommendation is to wait for a specific flush — a post-litigation-deadline washout, a weak first-half or Q2 2026 cash-flow print driven by seasonal patterns, or an AI-capex sell-off — to enter at the bear-case floor of $33–35, where the reward-to-risk ratio becomes overwhelming. Conviction 3 out of 5 (real thesis, catalyst-light, regime-neutral). Stripping out the catalyst and regime overlay, the pure value conviction is 4 out of 5: 6.5 times FCF on growing cash flow with a $55–60 sum-of-the-parts and aligned buybacks is genuinely attractive, docked one point only for the margin erosion, litigation, and insider selling — not a 5 because the AI-deflation question for the core business model is unresolved.
sop_breakdown:
The sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) base case reconciles the FCF-anchored bull scenario (~$73–80) and the AI-deflation bear scenario (~$33–35) into a probability-weighted ~$55–62, set just below the stale analyst consensus price target (~$78) and the more current May 2026 downgrade cluster ($44–59).

Core IT-services free cash flow stream: TTM FCF of $303 million, lightly normalized to ~$280 million, valued at roughly 9 times (midway between the 6.5 times trough multiple and peer EPAM's roughly 18 times; discounted for Argentine domicile and AI-deflation uncertainty) equals approximately $2.5 billion.

AI Pods optionality: the $60–100 million 2026-exit ARR target, valued at a conservative 4–5 times ARR and probability-adjusted, adds roughly $0.25–0.3 billion.

Gross enterprise value: approximately $2.75–2.85 billion. Subtract net debt of $262 million (a liability, never added to value) to arrive at equity value of roughly $2.5–2.6 billion. Divided by approximately 44.7 million shares, that implies $56–58 per share, rounded to a $55–60 base.

The buyback ($125 million through Q4 2027) is modestly accretive at the margin. An earnings power value (EPV, a no-growth valuation) cross-check at +19% margin of safety implies fair value of roughly $50–53 — slightly below this FCF-weighted base, but consistent.
risk_reward:
Downside to the bear-case floor: roughly $33–35 (approximately -15–20% from $42.57). Upside to base-case fair value: roughly $55–60 (+30–41%). Upside to bull case: roughly $73–80 (+71–88%).

The cash-flow asymmetry is favorable — approximately 2:1 reward-to-risk to the base case, approximately 4:1 to the bull case. However, realizing the upside requires a multi-quarter recovery in macro spending and pipeline conversion, or AI Pods scaling to materiality. There is no hard-dated forcing event, so the convergence path is slow. The litigation overhang and heavy insider selling argue for demanding the lower entry price rather than paying the current quote.
peer_comps_note:
The closest peer is EPAM Systems; the broader comparison set includes Cognizant Technology Solutions (CTSH), Wipro (WIT), Infosys (INFY), and Accenture (ACN) at the large-cap end. The comparison confirms Globant is cheap relative to peers: 6.5 times price-to-FCF versus EPAM at roughly 18 times.

The discount is partly warranted — Argentine domicile brings currency and political risk, the securities litigation is an ongoing overhang, EPS erosion has been faster than peers, and the 4.2% net margin is below the peer group. However, the discount appears to more than fully price the cyclical reset, given that the stock sits only 8% above its 52-week low. A re-rating to even 9–11 times FCF — still a wide discount to EPAM — supports the $55–60 base case. The implied roughly 9 times base multiple is conservative versus the comparison group and serves as the sanity check on the sum-of-the-parts valuation.
interrogator_dossier:
# Globant S.A. (GLOB) — Forensic Dossier

Interrogator dossier covering earnings call transcripts Q1 2025 (May 15, 2025) through Q4 2025 (February 26, 2026), cross-referenced to live full-year and trailing-twelve-month financials and June 2026 web verification. Signal type: deep value. Methodology: earnings power value (EPV, a conservative no-growth valuation).

## 1. Narrative Arc

The Globant story underwent a profound, three-act transformation across these four calls — from a wounded growth compounder pleading 'transitory,' to a company re-architecting its entire delivery model around AI.

Act I — Q1 2025 (May): 'Transitory macro shock.' CEO Martin Migoya frames a guidance cut as externally imposed: 'our Q1 performance came in below our initial expectations… we're currently operating in a challenging macroeconomic environment. The probability of a recession in the US has risen significantly… uncertainty from trade tariffs has impacted a good portion of our customers.' Constant-currency growth was still positive (+8.6%) but reported dollar growth stalled. Three forward pillars are introduced: the 100-square accounts program, AI studios, and — for the first time — the Globant Subscription Model (AI Pods), then a single-client pilot ('YPF has already adopted this model'). The tone is defensive ('protect cash flow,' 'lower capital expenditure,' 'more cautious on M&A… everything changed when the multiple changes').

Act II — Q2 2025 (August) and Q3 2025 (November): 'The pivot is real and accelerating.' AI Pods moves from footnote to centerpiece. Adoption metrics appear and compound: 'after just 1 quarter, we already have 18 clients' (Q2) to 'within our top 20 customers… we are embedding our subscription model with 17 of them' (Q3). Pipeline hits all-time highs ($3.7 billion, +25–30% year over year). Marquee logos are showcased — OpenAI global services partner, AWS strategic agreement, LaLiga, Riot Games ('one of the largest agreements in the history of our games business'), and Natura SAP S/4HANA implementation. A share buyback launches in Q3 ('reflecting how bullish we are'). The tone flips from defense to offense, but revenue stays stubbornly flat (~$614–617 million per quarter).

Act III — Q4 2025 (February 2026): 'AI-native company, AWS moment.' Full strategic re-identification: 'we are the AI native technology solutions company.' Migoya reaches for the boldest analogy yet — AI Pods as the 'AWS moment' for technology services, 'the foundation of an industry that does not yet fully exist.' Hard annual recurring revenue (ARR) numbers finally arrive: AI Pods exit-2025 ARR of $20.6 million, with gross margins of 45–60% versus the blended 38%, and a 2026 target of $60–100 million exit ARR. Crucially, management now concedes cannibalization openly: 'I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization. So I want that transformation to happen.'

What was quietly dropped: the 'transitory macro' framing of Q1 disappears entirely. By Q4, the slow growth is reframed as a structural industry shift (citing Gartner's IT-services growth estimate of +4.4%, described as an 'execution phase') rather than a passing storm — an implicit admission that the recovery never came.

## 2. Claims vs. Financials (Forensic)

Management claim vs. what the hard numbers say:

'Another solid quarter… healthy 8.6% year-over-year growth in constant currency' (Q1 2025): Full-year revenue grew only +1.6% in dollars ($2.42 billion to $2.45 billion); reported dollar growth was near flat. The constant-currency figure masked a near-stall in actual revenue — diverges from the narrative.

'Pipeline at all-time high $3.7 billion, +25%' through 'record bookings +32.4%' (Q2 through Q4 2025): Revenue went from $614 million to $617 million and then flat; full-year revenue grew 1.6%. Pipeline growth has not translated into revenue. Richer than reality — the bookings and pipeline narrative is not yet visible in the top line.

'Highest revenue and strongest FCF ever in 2025' (Q4 2025): TTM FCF of $303 million (10.6% FCF margin, +18.2% year over year); price-to-FCF of 6.5 times. The FCF claim is true. Matches — this is the one place the story is fully corroborated, and it is the linchpin of the earnings power value case.

AI Pods: 'this is a business, not an experiment' (Q4 2025): Exit ARR of $20.6 million equals less than 1% of $2.45 billion in revenue (an analyst on the Q4 call noted 'less than 1%'); Q1 2026 web check shows $32.8 million. Premature — real and growing fast, but immaterial to the 2026 income statement.

'Protect and generate more cash… lower capital expenditure' (Q1 2025): FCF margin held at 10.6%, FCF +18% year over year — delivered. Matches.

Margin defense / 'utilization toward 80–81%' (Q2 through Q4 2025): Net margin collapsed to 4.2% from a structurally higher base; earnings per share fell 38.4% year over year ($3.72 to $2.29); return on equity 8.0%, return on invested capital 6.9%. Diverges sharply — the profitability damage is severe and under-narrated on the calls.

The forensic core: the free cash flow story is honest and strong; the growth and margin story is materially worse than the relentless 'all-time-high pipeline' rhetoric implies. Earnings per share fell 38% while the pipeline hit records — the gap between the bookings narrative and earned profit is the central credibility tension.

## 3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Trajectory: declining then pivoting (defensive in Q1, offensive by reinvention in Q4).

Q1 2025 (defensive, rattled): 'Things got a lot worse and we were not able to offset those challenges… we cannot assume a meaningful recovery in the second half.' Migoya repeatedly hedged on guidance ('a number we feel is as safe as possible'). Confidence was visibly shaken by the tariff and recession shock.

Q2 2025 (rebuilding, evangelical): Energy returns via the AI narrative — 'two major races going on… this is not our race [foundation models]… the second is who applies AI better.' Confident on strategy, still vague on numbers.

Q3 2025 (assertive): 'We launched a share buyback… reflecting how bullish we are'; 'we are leading a revolution.' The most offensive tone of the four-quarter set.

Q4 2025 (visionary, all-in): The AWS analogy; '2026 is the year the market begins to validate that bet.' Maximum conviction on the pivot — but paired with a candid macro read (Gartner data, MIT pilot-failure data, $1.5–2 trillion in estimated technical debt) that is more sober and data-grounded than the vague 'transitory' framing of Q1.

The arc moves from offense (growth compounder) to defense (cash protection and macro caution) to a new offense (reinvention) — never returning to the old growth-compounder framing. Guidance became more specific over time (exact ARR targets in Q4), which is a credibility positive.

## 4. Guidance Credibility

Reliability: medium.

The Q1 2025 guidance cut was the defining miss. Prior guidance (~$623 million Q1 midpoint) was abandoned mid-year; management openly admitted 'things got a lot worse.' That was a genuine miss against their own bar.

Thereafter, management set the bar conservatively and cleared it: Q3 2025 revenue of $617.1 million came in '$2 million above our most recent guidance.' The screener's beat rate of 5 out of 7 quarters (71%) reflects this post-reset reliability.

Web verification (Q1 2026, reported May 14, 2026): revenue of $607.1 million beat guidance; non-IFRS earnings per share of $1.50 beat the $1.44 consensus; the full-year 2026 guide ($2.462–2.508 billion, 14–15% margin) was maintained, not cut. The serial-cut pattern appears to have stopped — management is now guiding conservatively and clearing the bar.

The credibility ding: the original February 2025 framing of Latin America as 'particularly beneficial' is now the subject of a securities class action (class period February 2024 through August 2025) alleging that management masked Latin American deterioration. That is a real strike against the historical candor of this same team.

## 5. Analyst Pressure Points

Sequential-growth math (Q1 2025, Arvind Ramnani, Piper Sandler): Pressed hard on the implied 500-basis-point turnaround from -5% in Q1 to flat in Q2 — 'is there enough optimism.' Migoya never gave a mechanistic answer, only 'we see stabilization.' The skepticism was vindicated: growth stayed flat all year.

AI Pods cannibalization and crossover (recurring, peaks Q4 2025): 'How do you prevent them from cannibalizing your core seat-based revenue… what's the revenue crossover point?' Management deflected the crossover question entirely — 'I don't have a number to provide' (Q3), 'I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization' (Q4). The single most important modeling question is the one they will not quantify.

Capital and liquidity (Q1 2025, Divya Goyal): Probing on cash protection and potential restructuring. Management denied restructuring plans ('that's not in the plan') — yet Q4 2025 then described having 'restructured our delivery organization,' a soft contradiction.

What analysts worry about that management is not addressing: the disconnect between record pipeline and flat revenue (conversion leakage), and Latin American-specific weakness — the very issue now litigated.

## 6. Risks and Positives

Key risks:

Earnings per share down 38% year over year ($3.72 to $2.29) and net margin at 4.2% — the profitability base eroded far faster than the earnings calls convey.

Securities class action (filed 2026, lead-plaintiff deadline June 23, 2026) alleging concealment of Latin American deterioration — a governance and candor overhang on this management team.

Pipeline-to-revenue conversion gap — three consecutive quarters of 'all-time-high pipeline' with flat revenue; the bookings narrative is outrunning earned dollars.

Insider selling (open-market, corrected data): 17 buys (146,000 shares) versus 16 sells (21.4 million shares) — the real conviction signal is heavily net-sold.

Argentine domicile — currency and political risk, wage freezes in Argentina and Mexico cited in the litigation.

Key positives:

FCF is genuinely strong and inexpensive — TTM FCF of $303 million, 10.6% margin, +18% year over year, price-to-FCF of 6.5 times. This is the real earnings power value anchor.

AI Pods ARR compounding fast — $20.6 million (year-end 2025) to $32.8 million (Q1 2026) at 45–60% gross margin versus 38% blended; structurally margin-accretive if it scales.

Two buybacks authorized — Q3 2025 program plus a fresh $125 million ($50 million per quarter through Q4 2027) announced May 14, 2026; management repurchasing its own equity at a trough multiple.

Guidance pattern stabilized — Q1 2026 beat and full-year guide maintained; the serial-cut era appears over.

Marquee AI partnerships — OpenAI, AWS, and NVIDIA global-partner status; Riot Games and LaLiga logos lend the pivot real substance.

## 7. Hidden Signals

Terminology migration: 'digital transformation' (Q1) to 'AI studios / AI Pods' (Q2–Q3) to 'AI-native technology solutions company' (Q4). The self-identification rewrite is the tell that management has bet the franchise on the pivot.

From hours to tokens: 'Delivery is limited in tokens' (Q1) becomes a full pricing philosophy by Q4 ('the customer owns everything, no seats, only usage'). Management is deliberately disrupting its own per-seat revenue model — bold and risky.

Capital-allocation posture hardened: Q1 'much more cautious / much more limited' on acquisitions ('everything changed when the multiple changes') to Q3–Q4 capital redirected to buybacks — an admission that the stock is the best asset the company can buy.

New voice: Chief Revenue Officer Fernando Matzkin was added to the Q4 earnings roster — revenue-leadership reinforcement amid the pipeline-conversion concerns.

The candid concession: 'AI has made it faster and more accessible to build… that is actually a demand accelerant' (Q4). Management is reframing the existential AI-deflation risk to labor-arbitrage IT services as a tailwind. Whether that reframing holds is the whole bull/bear question.

## 8. Capital-Allocation Assessment (for the portfolio director)

Classification: trajectory turning — from a stalled per-seat digital-transformation business into an AI-native, token-subscription services company, while the legacy income statement absorbs a severe margin and earnings-per-share reset.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: this is a deep-value-on-cash-flow case (6.5 times price-to-FCF, $303 million TTM FCF, twin buybacks) wrapped around an unproven, sub-1%-of-revenue AI Pods bet. The valuation does not need the pivot to work — but the re-rating catalyst depends on either (a) AI Pods scaling from $33 million to a material fraction of $2.45 billion in revenue, or (b) macro conditions and pipeline conversion turning so that the bookings finally translate into revenue. Both are soft, multi-quarter, single-axis (AI-capex and enterprise-spend) drivers, not hard-dated events. Size to the cash-flow margin of safety, not to the pivot narrative; treat the litigation overhang (June 23, 2026 deadline) and the heavy insider selling as reasons to demand a deeper discount, not to pay up for the story.

Management credibility 3 of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.

# Globant S.A. (GLOB) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator dossier · transcripts Q1'25 (2025-05-15) → Q4'25 (2026-02-26), cross-referenced to live FY/TTM financials and June-2026 web verification. Signal type: deep_value. Methodology: EPV.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The Globant story underwent a profound, three-act transformation across these four calls — from a wounded growth compounder pleading "transitory," to a company re-architecting its entire delivery model around AI.

**Act I — Q1'25 (May): "transitory macro shock."** Migoya frames a guidance cut as externally imposed: "our Q1 performance came in below our initial expectations… we're currently operating in a challenging macroeconomic environment. The probability of a recession in the US has risen significantly… uncertainty from trade tariffs has impacted a good portion of our customers." Constant-currency growth was still positive (+8.6% cc) but the reported number stalled. Three forward pillars are introduced: the 100-square accounts, AI studios, and — for the first time — the **Globant Subscription Model (AI Pods)**, then a single-client pilot ("YPF has already adopted this model"). The tone is defensive ("protect cash flow," "lower CapEx," "more cautious on M&A… everything changed when the multiple changes").

**Act II — Q2'25 (Aug) & Q3'25 (Nov): "the pivot is real and accelerating."** AI Pods moves from footnote to centerpiece. Adoption metrics appear and compound: "after just 1 quarter, we already have 18 clients" (Q2) → "within our top 20 customers… we are embedding our subscription model with 17 of them" (Q3). Pipeline hits all-time highs ($3.7B, +25-30% YoY). Marquee logos are paraded — OpenAI global services partner, AWS strategic agreement, LaLiga, Riot Games ("one of the largest agreements in the history of our games business"), Natura S/4HANA. A share buyback is launched in Q3 ("reflecting how bullish we are"). The framing flips from defense to offense, but revenue stays stubbornly flat (~$614-617M/qtr).

**Act III — Q4'25 (Feb'26): "AI-native company, AWS-moment."** Full strategic re-identification: "we are the AI native technology solutions company." Migoya reaches for the boldest analogy yet — AI Pods as the "AWS moment" for tech services, "the foundation of an industry that does not yet fully exist." Hard ARR numbers finally arrive: AI Pods exit-2025 ARR **$20.6M, gross margins 45-60% vs blended 38%**, with a 2026 target of **$60-100M exit ARR**. Crucially, management now concedes cannibalization openly: "I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization. So I want that transformation to happen."

**What was quietly dropped:** the "transitory macro" framing of Q1 disappears — by Q4 the slow growth is reframed as a *structural* industry shift (Gartner IT-services +4.4%, "execution phase") rather than a passing storm, an implicit admission the recovery never came.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The hard numbers say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "another solid quarter… healthy 8.6% YoY growth in constant currency" | Q1'25 | FY rev +1.6% YoY ($2.42B→$2.45B); USD-reported growth ~flat | **Diverges** — cc masks a near-stall in dollars |
| "pipeline at all-time high $3.7B, +25%" → "+30%" → "record bookings +32.4%" | Q2-Q4'25 | Revenue went $614→$617→flat; FY rev +1.6%. Pipeline growth ≠ revenue conversion | **Richer than reality** — bookings/pipeline narrative not yet visible in the top line |
| "highest revenue and strongest FCF ever in 2025" | Q4'25 | TTM FCF **$303M** (FCF margin 10.6%, +18.2% YoY); P/FCF **6.5x**. FCF claim TRUE | **Matches** — the one place the story is corroborated, and it's the linchpin of the EPV case |
| AI Pods "this is a business, not an experiment" | Q4'25 | $20.6M exit ARR = **<1% of $2.45B revenue** (analyst on Q4 call: "less than 1%"); Q1'26 web check: $32.8M | **Premature** — real and growing fast, but immaterial to 2026 P&L |
| "protect and generate more cash… lower CapEx" | Q1'25 | FCF margin held at 10.6%, FCF +18% YoY — delivered | **Matches** |
| Margin defense / "utilization toward 80-81%" | Q2-Q4'25 | Net margin **collapsed 4.2%** (from a structurally higher base); EPS **-38.4% YoY** ($3.72→$2.29); ROE 8.0%/ROIC 6.9% | **Diverges sharply** — the profitability damage is severe and under-narrated on the calls |

The forensic core: **the FCF/cash story is honest and strong; the growth and margin story is materially poorer than the relentless "all-time-high pipeline" rhetoric implies.** EPS fell 38% while pipeline hit records — the gap between bookings narrative and earned profit is the central credibility tension.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining-then-Pivoting (defensive Q1 → offensive-by-reinvention Q4).**

- **Q1'25 (defensive, rattled):** "things got a lot worse and we were not able to offset those challenges… we cannot assume a meaningful recovery in the second half." Migoya repeatedly hedges on guidance ("a number we feel is as safe as possible"). Confidence visibly shaken by the tariff/recession shock.
- **Q2'25 (rebuilding, evangelical):** energy returns via the AI narrative — "two major races going on… this is not our race [foundation models]… the second is who applies AI better." Confident on strategy, still vague on numbers.
- **Q3'25 (assertive):** "we launched a share buyback… reflecting how bullish we are"; "we are leading a revolu[tion]." Most offensive tone of the set.
- **Q4'25 (visionary, all-in):** the AWS analogy; "2026 is the year the market begins to validate that bet." Maximum conviction on the *pivot* — but paired with a candid macro read (Gartner, MIT pilot-failure data, $1.5-2T technical debt) that is more sober and data-grounded than the hand-wavy "transitory" of Q1.

The shift is from **offense (growth) → defense (cash/macro) → a NEW offense (reinvention)**, never returning to the old growth-compounder framing. Guidance became *more* specific over time (exact ARR targets in Q4), which is a credibility positive.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: Medium.**

- The Q1'25 guidance cut was the defining miss — prior guidance (~$623M Q1 midpoint) was abandoned mid-year; management openly admitted "things got a lot worse." This was a genuine miss against their own bar.
- Thereafter they **sandbagged deliberately and hit it:** Q3'25 revenue "$617.1M, $2 million above our most recent guidance" — beating a number they had reset low. The screener's beat rate (**5/7 qtrs, 71%**) reflects this post-reset reliability.
- Web verification (Q1'26, reported 2026-05-14): revenue **$607.1M beat guidance**, non-IFRS EPS $1.50 beat the $1.44 consensus; FY2026 guide ($2.462-2.508B, 14-15% margin) **maintained**, not cut. The serial-cut pattern appears to have stopped — management is now guiding conservatively and clearing the bar.
- The credibility ding: the *original* Feb-2025 framing of LatAm as "particularly beneficial" is now the subject of a securities class action (class period Feb-2024→Aug-2025) alleging they masked LatAm deterioration. That is a real strike against the *historical* candor of this same management team.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **Sequential-growth math (Q1'25, Arvind Ramnani, Piper):** pressed hard on the implied "500 bps turnaround" from -5% Q1 to flat Q2 — "is there enough optimism." Migoya never gave a mechanistic answer, only "we see stabilization." The skepticism was vindicated: growth stayed flat all year.
- **AI Pods cannibalization & crossover (recurring, peaks Q4'25):** "how do you prevent them from cannibalizing your core seat-based revenue… what's the revenue crossover point?" Management *deflected the crossover question entirely* — "I don't have a number to provide" (Q3), "I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization" (Q4). The single most important modeling question is the one they won't quantify.
- **Capital/liquidity (Q1'25, Divya Goyal):** probing on cash protection and restructuring. Management denied restructuring plans ("that's not in the plan") — yet Q4'25 then described having "restructured our delivery organization," a soft contradiction.
- **What analysts worry about that management isn't addressing:** the disconnect between record pipeline and flat revenue (conversion/leakage), and LatAm-specific weakness — the very thing now litigated.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **EPS down 38% YoY** ($3.72→$2.29) and net margin at 4.2% — the profitability base eroded far faster than the calls convey.
🔴 **Securities class action** (filed 2026, lead-plaintiff deadline 2026-06-23) alleging concealment of LatAm deterioration — a governance/candor overhang on *this* management team.
🔴 **Pipeline-to-revenue conversion gap** — three straight quarters of "all-time-high pipeline" with flat revenue; bookings narrative outrunning earned dollars.
🔴 **Insider selling (open-market, corrected data):** 17 buys (146k sh) vs 16 sells (21.4M sh) — the real conviction signal is heavily net-sold.
🔴 **Argentina domicile** — FX/political risk, wage freezes in Argentina & Mexico cited in the litigation.

🟢 **FCF is genuinely strong and cheap** — TTM FCF $303M, 10.6% margin, +18% YoY, P/FCF **6.5x**. This is the real EPV anchor.
🟢 **AI Pods ARR compounding fast** — $20.6M (exit-25) → $32.8M (Q1'26) at 45-60% gross margin vs 38% blended; structurally margin-accretive if it scales.
🟢 **Two buybacks authorized** — Q3'25 program + a fresh $125M ($50M/qtr through Q4'27) announced 2026-05-14; management buying its own equity at a trough multiple.
🟢 **Guidance pattern stabilized** — Q1'26 beat and FY guide maintained; the serial-cut era appears over.
🟢 **Marquee AI partnerships** — OpenAI/AWS/NVIDIA global-partner status, Riot/LaLiga logos lend the pivot real substance.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology migration:** "digital transformation" (Q1) → "AI studios / AI Pods" (Q2-Q3) → "**AI-native technology solutions company**" (Q4). The self-identification rewrite is the tell that management has bet the franchise on the pivot.
- **From hours to tokens:** "Delivery is limited in tokens" (Q1) becomes a full pricing philosophy by Q4 ("the customer owns everything, no seats, only usage"). They are deliberately disrupting their own per-seat revenue model — bold and risky.
- **Capital-allocation posture hardened:** Q1 "much more cautious / much more limited" on M&A ("everything changed when the multiple changes") → by Q3-Q4 capital is redirected to **buybacks**, an admission the stock is the best asset they can buy.
- **New voice:** CRO Fernando Matzkin added to the Q4 roster — revenue-leadership reinforcement amid the conversion concerns.
- **The candid concession:** "AI has made it faster and more accessible to build… that is actually a demand accelerant" (Q4) — management is reframing the existential AI-deflation risk to labor-arbitrage IT services as a tailwind. Whether that holds is the whole bull/bear axis.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING** — from a stalled per-seat digital-transformation compounder into an AI-native, token-subscription services company, while the legacy P&L absorbs a severe margin/EPS reset.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** this is a **deep-value-on-cash-flow case (6.5x P/FCF, $303M TTM FCF, twin buybacks) wrapped around an unproven, sub-1%-of-revenue AI Pods bet** — the valuation does NOT need the pivot to work, but the *re-rate catalyst* depends on either (a) AI Pods scaling from $33M to a material fraction of $2.45B, or (b) macro/conversion turning so the pipeline finally converts. Both are soft, multi-quarter, single-axis (AI-capex/enterprise-spend) drivers, not a hard-dated event. Size to the cash-flow margin of safety, not to the pivot narrative; respect the litigation overhang (2026-06-23 deadline) and the heavy insider selling as evidence to *demand a deeper discount*, not to pay up for the story.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING

moderator_conclusion: PASS (C), conviction 2. interrogator_dossier:
# Globant (GLOB) — Forensic Interrogator Dossier

*Inputs: Q1–Q4 2025 earnings transcripts (2025-05-15, 2025-08-14, 2025-11-13, 2026-02-26), screener financial trajectory, and live web verification as of 2026-06-17.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story management told across 2025 underwent a near-total rewrite — not a refinement, a **pivot**, and the pivot is itself the tell.

- **Q1 2025 (May):** The frame was *defensive damage control*. Management cut guidance hard ("our Q1 performance came in below our initial expectations"), blamed an exogenous macro shock ("the probability of a recession in the US has risen significantly," "uncertainty from trade tariffs has impacted a good portion of our customers"), and insisted the weakness was "transitory." Capital priorities flipped overnight from growth/M&A to cash protection: "make our CapEx investments lower," "extending DSO payment terms," "much more cautious… much more limited" on M&A. Crucially, Migoya conceded the multiple re-rate is permanent: *"Everything changed when the multiple changes. So we have a new reality there."* AI Pods were introduced as a brand-new consumption/subscription concept ("YPF has already adopted this model").

- **Q2 2025 (Aug):** Pivot accelerates. Pipeline is recast as the hero metric ("all-time high, $3.7 billion, up 25%"), explicitly to paper over flat revenue. AI Pods go from a footnote to the centerpiece ("18 clients… a significant portion of our recent pipeline growth"). The OpenAI and AWS partnerships are unveiled. The framing shifts to "the second race… who applies AI better" — a re-positioning *away* from being a foundational-model laggard and *toward* being the indispensable integrator.

- **Q3 2025 (Nov):** Full AI-native rebrand. A buyback is launched ("how bullish we are"). Subscription model is now embedded in "17 of [top 20 customers]." The narrative leans on TAM ("$4.8 trillion by 2033") and the AWS-creation-myth analogy ("Just as Amazon transformed the technology landscape…").

- **Q4 2025 (Feb 2026):** The pivot is complete and explicit: *"that layer is already changing our trajectory"* and *"we believe Globant is the AI native technology solutions company."* For the first time, hard AI Pods economics are disclosed — 2025 exit ARR **$20.6M**, gross margins 45–60% vs 38% blended, 2026 target **$60–100M exit ARR**. Management openly embraces cannibalization: *"I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization. So I want that transformation to happen."*

**The arc in one line:** a 20%+ CAGR growth compounder discovered its growth engine had stalled, reframed the stall as a deliberate "AI-native transformation," and substituted *pipeline* and *ARR run-rate* for the *revenue growth* it can no longer deliver. Growth drivers quietly dropped: the historic 20%+ organic engine and the LatAm "$1B pivot" (now the subject of a securities suit). Drivers introduced: AI Pods, subscription ARR, token economics.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | The financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "another solid quarter… healthy 8.6% YoY growth constant currency" | Q1'25 | FY2025 revenue **+1.6% YoY** ($2.42B→$2.45B); reported Q growth went negative ("Q1, it was negative 5%") | **Diverges.** The "8.6% CC" leans entirely on the FX/CC adjustment; reported reality is a stall. |
| "highest revenue and strongest free cash flow generation ever" (2025) | Q4'25 | Revenue technically true (+1.6%) but NI **collapsed -38.4%** ($166M→$103M); EPS $3.72→$2.29. FCF margin 10.6%, TTM FCF $303M is real | **Mixed.** FCF claim corroborates (TTM FCF $303M, P/FCF 6.3x). "Highest revenue ever" is technically true but masks the earnings collapse beneath it. |
| Pipeline "$3.7B up 25%"… "$3.4B"… record bookings "+32.4%" | Q2–Q4'25 | Pipeline grew while revenue went flat (+1.6%) and **forward consensus EPS growth is only +6.3%** | **Diverges — the core tell.** Pipeline up 25–30% for four straight quarters has NOT converted to revenue. Bookings/pipeline are being used as a substitute for the growth the P&L won't show. |
| "fine-tuning the organization to be more efficient and profitable" | Q2'25 | Gross margin **35.0% (stable)**, blended GM disclosed at **38%** and falling toward AI Pods mix; net margin **4.2%**; ROIC **6.9%** (< WACC) | **Poorer than narrative.** Margins are stable-to-eroding, not improving. ROIC below cost of capital means the "efficiency" story is not yet showing in returns. |
| AI Pods "is not an experiment. This is a business." | Q4'25 | 2025 exit ARR **$20.6M**; March-2026 ARR **$32.8M** (per live data) = **~1.3% of $2.46B revenue** | **Aspirational.** Real and growing, but a rounding error on the base. The 2026 $60–100M target is still <4% of revenue. The thesis is a multi-year bet, not a current earnings driver. |
| "launched a share buyback… how bullish we are" | Q3'25 | New **$125M** authorization announced Q1'26 (~3% of ~$1.6B mkt cap). Insiders (open-market): 17 buys / **146k shares** vs 16 sells / **21.4M shares** | **Contradicted by insiders.** The open-market insider signal is overwhelmingly net-SELLING (21.4M sold vs 0.15M bought). The buyback "bullishness" is not matched by personal conviction. |
| Macro weakness is "transitory" | Q1'25 | One year later (Q4'25→FY26 guide) growth is **still 0.3–2.2%**; Jefferies models **0.5% organic CC**, downgraded to Hold | **Refuted by time.** What was called transitory in Q1'25 is now a structural low-single-digit regime four quarters later. |

**Net:** the narrative is consistently *richer* than the trajectory. Every soft revenue print is offset rhetorically with a pipeline/ARR/CC number that does not flow to the P&L. The one place the numbers genuinely corroborate is **free cash flow** (TTM FCF $303M, FCF margin 10.6%, P/FCF 6.3x) — the deep-value anchor is real even as the growth story is not.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (then re-inflated rhetorically).**

- **Q1'25 — defensive, apologetic.** "below our initial expectations," "challenging macroeconomic environment," "we cannot assume a meaningful recovery." Migoya repeatedly hedges guidance: *"a number that we feel is as safe as possible,"* *"a number again that at least we can meet."* This is sandbag-and-survive language.
- **Q2'25 — pivoting, deflecting to pipeline.** Confidence is asserted but relocated from revenue to pipeline ("all-time high"). The shift from *offensive* (we will grow) to *defensive-repositioned* (we are transforming, watch the pipeline) is complete here.
- **Q3'25 — promotional.** TAM citations, AWS analogy, buyback-as-confidence-signal. The rhetoric inflates as the fundamentals stay flat.
- **Q4'25 — maximally promotional.** "changing our trajectory," "the AI native technology solutions company." Highest-conviction *language* of the four calls — but layered over a -38% NI year and a 0.3–2.2% forward guide.

**The pattern:** confidence in *words* rose across the year while confidence in *guidance* fell (low-single-digit, "no material improvement assumed"). That divergence — rising narrative volume over a falling earnings base — is the classic late-cycle promotional tell. Management got *less* specific on the one number that matters (when does revenue growth return?) and *more* specific on the aspirational one (ARR exit-rate targets).

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-LOW.**

- The Q1'25 guidance cut was the credibility-defining event: prior guidance ("$623M at the midpoint") was abandoned within a quarter, and management admitted "things got a lot worse and we were not able to offset those challenges." That is a real miss against their own prior path.
- Since then they have hit the *lowered* bar — Q3'25 revenue "$2M above our most recent guidance," Q1'26 "$607.1M, exceeding guidance." But this is beating a deliberately de-risked number ("a number that at least we can meet"), i.e., **sandbagging post-reset**, not genuine over-delivery.
- The screener's beat-rate (5/7 quarters, 71%) is consistent with a company that resets low and clears the lowered bar — respectable mechanically, but the bar itself stepped down materially.
- The deepest credibility wound is external: the **securities class action** (Ohio Carpenters' Pension Fund, filed 2026-04-24, SDNY) alleging the company *misled investors about its LatAm operations* between Feb-2024 and Aug-2025 — i.e., during the exact "transitory macro" framing period. This directly attacks the truthfulness of the 2024–25 narrative.

Score: **Low-to-Medium.** They deliver on lowered guidance; they did not deliver on the original trajectory, and the honesty of the earlier story is now in litigation.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"What gets you back to prior guidance?" (Piper Sandler, Q1'25):** Migoya could not name a company-controlled driver — the answer was "if those things go away," "if things get better," i.e., the recovery is outsourced to macro. Arvind Ramnani pressed the math directly: *"-5% in Q1… now Q2 you're going to be flat… that implies a 500bps turnaround"* — management non-answered with "stabilization." The skepticism was correct: a year later growth is still ~0.5%.
- **Cannibalization (Q4'25):** "how do you prevent [AI Pods] from cannibalizing your core seat-based revenue?" — Migoya's answer ("I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization… I want that transformation to happen") is honest but confirms the bear's core fear: **the new model deflates headline revenue.** Urthiague: "not necessarily AI Pods are cheaper, they are more productive" — i.e., fewer billable hours per outcome.
- **Crossover point (Q4'25):** asked when token-revenue overtakes headcount-revenue, management explicitly declined to model it ("I don't have a number to provide to you"). The single most important question for the thesis — when does the transition stop being a revenue headwind — is unanswered.
- **Vendor consolidation / pricing (UBS, Q2'25):** management claimed wins in consolidations but the pricing subtext (clients "tired of traditional big firms," "softer vendor selection") points to a competitive, price-pressured environment.

The through-line: analysts kept pressing on *when revenue growth returns and whether AI Pods cannibalize the base*, and management never gave a dated, quantified answer. Those deflections were the early warning of the FY2026 0.3–2.2% guide and the Jefferies Hold downgrade.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Revenue growth collapse from 20%+ CAGR to ~1.6%** (FY25), guided to 0.3–2.2% FY26 — a structural deceleration, not a blip.
🔴 **Net income -38.4% YoY** (FY25, $166M→$103M); EPS $3.72→$2.29 — earnings power impaired even as "revenue ever" hits a record.
🔴 **Insider open-market selling 21.4M shares vs 146k bought** — the real conviction signal flatly contradicts the buyback "bullishness."
🔴 **Securities class action** (SDNY, Apr-2026) alleging misrepresentation of LatAm operations during the very period management called the weakness "transitory."
🔴 **Pipeline-as-substitute:** four straight quarters of "+25–30% pipeline / record bookings" with zero revenue conversion — a textbook narrative-over-numbers pattern.
🔴 **ROIC 6.9% < WACC** and falling NI — value is being held by FCF, not by returns on capital.
🔴 **AI is the threat dressed as the opportunity:** management's own model (token-based, "more productive," cannibalizing seats) *deflates* the labor-arbitrage revenue base. Accenture is cutting 11k staff on the same force.

🟢 **Free cash flow is genuinely strong and improving:** TTM FCF $303M, FCF margin 10.6%, FCF +18.2% YoY, P/FCF 6.3x — the deep-value anchor is real.
🟢 **AI Pods gross margins 45–60% vs 38% blended** — *if* the mix scales, it is margin-accretive; ARR $20.6M→$32.8M in two quarters is real traction.
🟢 **Net debt manageable** ($262M live; $725M facility) — no maturity wall, no solvency crisis (Altman-Z 2.4).
🟢 **Sticky enterprise relationships** (49 clients >$10M, up from 39; top-5 grew +2.1% sequentially) and consolidation wins — the franchise hasn't broken, it's stalled.
🟢 **Buyback ($125M new authorization)** + cheap multiple = a real, if modest, capital-return floor.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Vocabulary migration from "growth" to "transformation."** Across four calls the word "growth" is increasingly attached to *pipeline* and *bookings*, never to *revenue*. By Q4'25 the operative noun is "trajectory" — a softer, longer-horizon word that excuses near-term flatness.
- **M&A posture flip.** From an acquisitive serial-consolidator to "much more limited… cautious," explicitly because "the multiple changes." This is management quietly admitting the inorganic growth flywheel (which had inflated the historic CAGR) is off.
- **The "two races" reframe (Q2'25).** Migoya pre-empts the bear case ("this is not our race… [foundational models]") and re-positions Globant as the picks-and-shovels integrator. This is a defensive narrative move — conceding they cannot win the model layer, betting they're indispensable at the application layer.
- **Cannibalization embraced, not resisted.** Management *wants* the transition that deflates revenue — a bet that productivity/margin and stickiness beat headline growth. Honest, but it means the revenue line is structurally capped for the duration of the transition with no dated end.
- **CRO addition (Q4'25, Fernando Matzkin joins Q&A).** A new Chief Revenue Officer surfaced exactly as the revenue problem became the central issue — an organizational tell that converting pipeline to revenue is now the #1 internal priority.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (under structural pressure).** This is not a clean STRENGTHENING (returns and growth are falling) nor a clean DETERIORATING (FCF is strong, franchise intact, balance sheet fine). It is a genuine pivot — from labor-arbitrage growth compounder to AI-native subscription model — executed *because* the old engine stalled, with the new engine still <1.5% of revenue.

**The single thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *Is the AI transition a margin-accretive moat-rebuild that the market is mispricing at 6.3x FCF — or is it a polite name for terminal labor-arbitrage deflation, where AI Pods cannibalize the seat-based base faster than they scale, leaving a structurally-shrinking revenue line that no amount of FCF yield can offset?* Management's own words ("I want that cannibalization to happen," "not necessarily cheaper," can't model the crossover) lean toward the latter as the near-term reality. The cheapness is real; the question is whether it is a fear-print on a stable FCF base or a value trap on a melting one.

**MOAT.** Type: **intangible-brand / switching-costs (weak form)** — design-led differentiation, embedded enterprise relationships, 100-square accounts. But the WIDTH evidence cuts against width: **ROIC 6.9% (below WACC) and falling**, **net margin 4.2%**, **NI -38% YoY**, gross margin flat-to-eroding (38% blended drifting toward AI Pods mix). A moat that cannot earn its cost of capital and whose returns are *falling* is, by the evidence, **ERODING**, not wide. The relationships are sticky; the *economics* of those relationships are deflating.

**SECULAR THREAT.** The structural force is **AI displacement of IT-services labor arbitrage** — the exact mechanism management is *building into its own model*. Revenue is being decoupled from headcount ("not necessarily cheaper, more productive"), bill-rates and hours per outcome compress, and the industry leader (Accenture) is cutting 11k staff on the same wave. This is a **material-to-terminal** threat: it directly caps the pricing power and ARPU of the legacy base. It is *not* purely cyclical — the "transitory macro" framing has already failed the time test (still ~0.5% growth a year later). The cheapness is therefore best read as a **structurally-shrinking-base value trap risk**, only partly offset by a real FCF anchor and a genuine (but tiny) margin-accretive AI Pods optionality. A low multiple on a base whose terminal value is being eroded by the very technology the company sells is the definition of a value trap — though the strong FCF and net-cash-light balance sheet keep it from being capital-destruction.

---

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: ERODING | MOAT_TREND: ERODING | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis: GLOB is a genuinely cash-generative, ex-growth IT-services franchise priced as if in terminal decline when the cash evidence says otherwise. TTM FCF $303M (10.6% margin, +18% YoY) on a ~$1.5B cap = ~5.3x P/FCF and a ~20% trailing FCF yield; gross margin is stable at 35%, so the delivery engine is intact and the damage is in operating leverage and growth, not cost of delivery. Five independent valuation methods corroborate cheapness (DCF +52%/$63.9, Buffett/OwnerEarn +58%/$78.5, EPV +37%, IV15 +32%, Graham +14%) and GLOB is the cheapest name in the IT-services peer cohort (EPAM, CTSH, ACN, INFY) near its 52-week low. Tentative stabilization is visible: TTM EPS $2.46 vs FY25 $2.29, latest-Q EPS +25% YoY, Q1-26 revenue $607.1M above the high end of guide, with the lower end of the FY range raised and management calling Q1 the trough. Free optionality sits in the mix: Data & AI is the #2 studio growing >25% YoY and AI-Pods ARR is compounding ($20.6M->$32.8M, $352M pipeline, 45-60% gross margins vs 38% blended) for which the market pays nothing. Capital return is shareholder-friendly: completed $125M buyback plus a fresh $125M authorization (~15% of cap across both plans) at a depressed price, M&A throttled. Holds if Q1 was the trough, AI-native/Data-&-AI growth offsets bill-rate deflation in the legacy book, FCF holds near $300M, and the US/tariff pipeline overhang eases.
bear_thesis: GLOB is the canonical AI-displacement value trap: a labor-arbitrage IT-services shop whose hour-billing-at-a-spread economics are deflated by the agentic-AI it sells, on a base that has already gone ex-growth. EPS fell 38% (3.72->2.29), revenue is flat-to-down (+1.6% FY25, Q1-26 -0.7% YoY, FY26 guide 0.2-2.2% and FX-aided), net margin compressed from ~7-8% to 4.2% (-1.72 sigma below peak), and ROIC 6.9% / ROE 8.0% sit below WACC — value destruction at the margin and an ERODING, not wide, moat. The cheapness is printed on a melting base. The CEO is the thesis: asked how Pods avoid cannibalizing seat revenue he said 'I'm not in a position to prevent cannibalization. So I want that transformation to happen,' and repeatedly refused a revenue-mix/crossover number. AI-Pods ARR ($32.8M) is ~1.3% of revenue — a rounding error that cannot offset deflation in the 98.7% legacy book; even the 2026 target is 2.5-4%. Pipeline is a vanity metric that grew to $3.7B while revenue stalled then rolled to $3.4B; conversion is the bind and management's stated levers are macro-rescue ('if interest rates go down'). The 'forward-deployed engineer' relabels consulting and pushes on-site delivery (margin-negative vs offshore arbitrage). Governance is impaired: an April-24-2026 SDNY securities class action alleges GLOB misled investors on a $1B LatAm growth engine that contracted 9% (headcount -1,000, troubled Iteris/Brazil integration); the stock fell $210->$66 over the class period. Insiders are net distributors (16 sells/21.4M sh vs 17 buys/146k sh). Net debt $262M (not cash) and Altman-Z 2.3 mean no fortress to wait inside. Confirmed by: another flat/down quarter invalidating the trough call, net revenue-per-client falling as Pods cannibalize T&M, FCF rolling as DSO/CapEx levers exhaust, and continued PT cuts (UBS $75->$50, Truist $54->$44).
consensus_delta: For a deep_value name the question is whether the street is pricing terminal decline into a company with stable/growing earnings power. Here the answer is NO — the street is, if anything, only now catching down to a thesis that is structurally bearish, and the cheap optics overstate the mispricing. The would-be variant view (bulls + the screener's $54.56 consensus / DCF $63.9 / owner-earnings $78.5) assumes (1) Q1-26 was a genuine trough and revenue re-accelerates, and (2) AI-Pods/AI-native layer offsets bill-rate deflation in the legacy book. Both are contradicted by the evidence: revenue is ex-growth (Q1-26 -0.7% YoY, FY26 guide 0.2-2.2% FX-aided), AI-Pods ARR is ~1.3% of revenue, and the CEO conceded he cannot prevent or model net cannibalization. The real expectations gap is the OPPOSITE of the classic deep-value setup: consensus PTs are still being CUT toward reality (UBS $75->$50, Truist $54->$44, Needham $60->$50 post-Q1), not anchored too high on stale optimism. The mistake the street is making is the residual DCF/owner-earnings fair values ($64-78) that extrapolate a maintainable earnings base the secular thesis rejects. Net: there is no offside long-side pain trade to harvest here — the cheapness is correctly suspected, the discount is shared sector AI-displacement beta, and the variant-perception edge is thin to negative.
forcing_function: For deep_value the forcing function is the event that makes the market re-rate stable earnings power. Here the candidate events are weak and/or adverse. (a) Q2-26 earnings (~mid-Aug-2026) — the first hard test of the 'trough' call; this is the only dated binding event, and it is a two-sided binary that more likely confirms deceleration than forces an up-rerate. (b) AI-Pods 2026 exit-ARR (target $60-100M) — reported quarterly, but at 2.5-4% of revenue it is too small to force anything for years. (c) The $125M buyback executes at management discretion (no tender, no fixed window) — a price support, not a forcing function. (d) The securities class action timeline is a forcing function in the WRONG direction (overhang, not catalyst). There is no activist, no asset sale, no spin, no dividend initiation, no dated structural special-situation — i.e., none of the hard-dated, thin-coverage catalysts the current regime rewards. The thesis hinges on a self-help pivot plus a macro rescue the regime explicitly penalizes. No forcing function with real asymmetry exists.
valley_of_death: Near-term (3-9mo) hazards that hit before any re-rate. (1) The 'Q1 marks the trough' call is unproven and binary: Q2-26 prints (guide $610-616M) and FY26 (0.2-2.2% growth) must actually show sequential improvement; a single flat/down quarter invalidates the entire bull narrative and re-opens the de-rate. (2) Net revenue-per-client erosion as AI Pods cannibalize T&M with no proven offset — the CEO's 'I want that transformation to happen' means management is actively accelerating the cannibalization. (3) FCF durability risk: the strong $303M TTM FCF was partly engineered via CapEx cuts and DSO extension (mgmt's own Q1-25 words) — those levers exhaust, and a no-growth services book cannot indefinitely out-earn its P&L.
